title: Iran-US tensions push crude oil prices to new highs, India braces for impact
text: Escalating tensions between Iran and the United States have pushed global crude oil prices to their highest levels in over a year, with Brent crude surging past $95 per barrel. The conflict, centered around Iran's nuclear program and US sanctions, has raised fears of a potential disruption to oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz, through which approximately 20% of the world's oil supply transits daily. India, which imports roughly 85% of its crude oil needs, stands to be significantly affected. Analysts at Goldman Sachs warn that a sustained oil price spike could add 1.5-2 percentage points to India's consumer price inflation and widen the current account deficit by $15-20 billion annually. The Indian rupee has already weakened 2.3% against the US dollar this month, pressured by rising oil import bills. The Reserve Bank of India expressed concern about imported inflation feeding through to domestic prices. India's petroleum ministry has begun discussions with Saudi Arabia and UAE for emergency supply arrangements. Meanwhile, India's industrial production index showed signs of stress, with manufacturing output growth slowing to 2.1% amid rising input costs driven by energy prices.

title: Strait of Hormuz shipping insurance costs spike amid Iran military exercises
text: Shipping insurance premiums for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz have spiked by 300% following Iran's announcement of naval military exercises near the strategic waterway. The Strait of Hormuz is the world's most important oil chokepoint, with approximately 21 million barrels per day flowing through the narrow passage. Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps conducted live-fire drills within 50 nautical miles of commercial shipping lanes, prompting several tanker operators to reroute vessels. India, which depends on the Strait for over 60% of its oil imports and significant LNG shipments, faces elevated supply chain risks. Indian Oil Corporation has reportedly activated contingency procurement plans, seeking alternative supplies from West African producers. OPEC has stated it will monitor the situation closely but has not announced emergency production increases. The disruption threat has also affected natural gas markets, with Asian LNG spot prices rising 15% in the past week.

title: India's inflation surges to 7.2% as oil price shock ripples through economy
text: India's consumer price inflation accelerated to 7.2% year-on-year in November, breaching the Reserve Bank of India's upper tolerance band of 6%, driven primarily by surging energy costs linked to the Iran-US standoff. The wholesale price index showed even sharper increases, with fuel and power components rising 18.5%. Transportation costs have increased across the board as diesel and petrol prices reached record highs. Industrial production growth contracted for the first time in 18 months, with the Index of Industrial Production declining 0.3% in October. Small and medium enterprises in energy-intensive sectors such as chemicals, ceramics, and textiles reported severe margin compression. The rupee touched a historic low of 86.5 against the US dollar, reflecting India's worsening trade balance. India's current account deficit is projected to widen to 3.5% of GDP if oil prices remain elevated. The government is considering releasing strategic petroleum reserves and temporarily cutting excise duty on fuel to cushion the impact on consumers.

title: OPEC emergency meeting considers production boost amid Hormuz crisis
text: OPEC has called an emergency ministerial meeting to discuss potential production increases in response to the escalating crisis in the Strait of Hormuz region. Saudi Arabia, the cartel's de facto leader, has signaled willingness to increase output by up to 1 million barrels per day to stabilize global markets. The move comes as crude oil prices remain above $90 per barrel due to the ongoing Iran-USA military confrontation. The United States has deployed additional naval assets to the Persian Gulf, while Iran continues to conduct military exercises near commercial shipping routes. India's External Affairs Minister has called for diplomatic de-escalation, noting India's critical dependence on uninterrupted energy supplies from the Gulf region. Iraqi oil exports, which transit through the Persian Gulf, account for approximately 23% of India's crude imports. Any disruption would force India to seek costlier alternatives from distant suppliers, further straining the already pressured Indian rupee and widening the fiscal deficit.

title: India explores alternative energy routes as Gulf tensions persist
text: As tensions between Iran and the United States continue to disrupt energy markets, India is actively exploring alternative supply routes and accelerating its renewable energy transition. The government has fast-tracked negotiations with Russia for increased Urals crude supply via the northern sea route and expanded pipeline capacity from Central Asian gas fields. India's oil import bill has surged 35% year-on-year, contributing to a 2.8% depreciation of the Indian rupee against the US dollar in the past quarter alone. The Ministry of Petroleum reported that strategic petroleum reserves at Visakhapatnam, Mangalore, and Padur hold approximately 39 days of net imports, providing a limited buffer. Meanwhile, India's industrial production in energy-intensive sectors has declined, with the cement industry reporting a 5% output drop and steel production falling 3% due to elevated input costs. The Federation of Indian Chambers of Commerce warned that sustained high energy prices could reduce GDP growth by 0.5-0.8 percentage points in the current fiscal year. LNG imports from Qatar, which also transit through the Strait of Hormuz, face delivery uncertainties, prompting Indian gas utilities to secure emergency spot cargoes from Australia and the US.
